---
title: Tera Functions
---

::: {.callout-tip}
Click the `</>` button in the top navigation bar to view the original `.qmd` source code.
:::

*Calepin* processes the body of `.qmd` documents as [Tera](https://keats.github.io/tera/) templates. This means you can use Tera expressions, functions, conditionals, and loops directly in your text. Code blocks and inline code are automatically protected from Tera evaluation.


# Syntax

Tera expressions use double braces: `{% raw %}{{ expression }}{% endraw %}`. Functions use parentheses with keyword arguments:

```
{% raw %}{{ function_name(arg="value") }}{% endraw %}
```

To show Tera syntax literally without processing it, wrap it in raw blocks:

````
{% raw %}{% raw %}{{ this is not evaluated }}{% endraw %}{% endraw %}
````


# Context variables

## meta

Access YAML front matter fields directly:

{% raw %}
- `{{ meta.title }}` --- document title
- `{{ meta.author }}` --- author (comma-joined if multiple)
- `{{ meta.date }}` --- date
- `{{ meta.subtitle }}` --- subtitle
- `{{ meta.abstract }}` --- abstract text
- `{{ meta.keywords }}` --- keywords (comma-joined)
{% endraw %}

Any custom field in the YAML front matter is also available.

The title of this document is: {{ meta.title }}.

## var

Look up a variable from `_variables.yml` using dot-notation. For example, this project's `_variables.yml` contains:

```yaml
version: 0.0.1-alpha.2
urls:
  docs: https://github.com/vincentarelbundock/calepin
```

{% raw %}The syntax `{{ var.version }}` inserts the value.{% endraw %}

* *Calepin* version: {{ var.version }}
* Documentation URL: {{ var.urls.docs }}

## format

The current output format is available as `{% raw %}{{ format }}{% endraw %}` --- one of `html`, `latex`, `typst`, or `markdown`.


# Built-in functions

## pagebreak

Insert a native page break. Produces format-appropriate output: `<div style="page-break-after: always;">` in HTML, `\newpage{}` in LaTeX, `#pagebreak()` in Typst.

Page one content.

{% raw %}`{{ pagebreak() }}`{% endraw %}

Page two content.

## env

Print a system environment variable. If the variable is not set, produces an empty string.

{% raw %}`{{ env(name="SHELL") }}`{% endraw %}

The `SHELL` environment variable on the local machine is: {{ env(name="SHELL") }}.

## video

Embed a video or streaming service. Supports YouTube, Vimeo, and direct video URLs:

```
{% raw %}{{ video(url="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ") }}
{{ video(url="https://vimeo.com/123456", width="640", height="480", title="My Video") }}{% endraw %}
```

YouTube and Vimeo URLs are automatically converted to embed URLs.

## brand

Access branding information from `_brand.yml`:

{% raw %}
- `{{ brand(type="color", name="primary") }}` --- returns a color value
- `{{ brand(type="logo", name="mark") }}` --- returns format-appropriate logo markup
{% endraw %}

## kbd

Render keyboard shortcuts:

{% raw %}`{{ kbd(keys=["Ctrl", "C"]) }}`{% endraw %}

Produces `<kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>C</kbd>` in HTML, `\texttt{Ctrl}+\texttt{C}` in LaTeX.

## include

Include the content of another file. Uses Tera's include tag syntax: `{%` `include "file.qmd"` `%}`.

This is a pre-parse directive (not a Tera function) because included content must be parsed as blocks. YAML front matter in the included file is stripped automatically. Code chunks, fenced divs, and other block structures in the included file are processed normally.


# Tera features in documents

Because the body is a Tera template, you can use conditionals and loops:

````
{% raw %}{% if format == "html" %}
This paragraph only appears in HTML output.
{% endif %}{% endraw %}
````

````
{% raw %}{% for item in var.features %}
- {{ item }}
{% endfor %}{% endraw %}
````

See the [Tera documentation](https://keats.github.io/tera/docs/) for the full template syntax.


# Plugin shortcodes

Custom functions are implemented as plugins with a `shortcode` capability. They are registered as Tera functions automatically.

## Example: greeting plugin

`_calepin/plugins/greeting/plugin.yml`:

```yaml
name: greeting
version: 0.1.0
description: "A friendly greeting shortcode"

provides:
  shortcode:
    run: shortcode.py
    names: [greeting]
```

`_calepin/plugins/greeting/shortcode.py`:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import json, sys

data = json.load(sys.stdin)
name = data["args"][0] if data["args"] else "World"
fmt = data["format"]

if fmt == "html":
    print(f'<strong>Hello, {name}!</strong>')
elif fmt == "tex":
    print(f'\\textbf{{Hello, {name}!}}')
elif fmt == "typ":
    print(f'Hello, {name}!')
else:
    print(f'Hello, {name}!')
```

{% raw %}Use it as `{{ greeting(name="Alice") }}`.{% endraw %}

## Input protocol

The executable receives JSON on stdin:

```json
{
  "name": "greeting",
  "args": [],
  "kwargs": {"name": "Alice"},
  "format": "html",
  "meta": {
    "title": "My Document",
    "author": ["Author Name"],
    "date": "2025-01-01"
  }
}
```

## Output protocol

Print the replacement text to stdout. The output is inserted directly into the document at the function call location.

- Exit 0: stdout replaces the function call.
- Exit non-zero: a warning is printed, and the function call produces an empty string.

See [Plugins](plugins.qmd) for the full plugin system documentation, including how to scaffold a plugin with `calepin plugin init`.


# Resolution order

For each function call, *Calepin* checks:

1. Built-in functions (`pagebreak`, `env`, `video`, `brand`, `kbd`)
2. Context variables (`meta`, `var`, `format`)
3. Plugin registry (in front matter order)

Built-in functions take precedence. You cannot override them with plugins.
